Insiders and Sentiment

Insider activity is also an important indicator. Several senior officials purchased shares of the company at low points, showing confidence in the company’s direction. These were not token purchases, given that they took place while the market outlook was cautiously optimistic.

Institutional sentiment, on the other hand, remains tempered. Coverage is shallow for such a small food group company, and most generalist institutional funds have preferred staples with higher levels of liquidity. There is also a clear divergence between underlying institutional interest and the still-muted sentiment reflected in the share price.

Catalysts On the Horizon

Several things could drive changes in perception for investors. One would be EBITDA margin expansion. With operational improvements passing through, the market will have to think about the company’s core earnings opportunities. Another is geographic expansion. Success in California and other less penetrated markets will simply prove its nationalization plan is viable. Another factor would be normalized free cash flow, which reflects normalized capital expenditures. And, finally, there is industry consolidation – it could be an optional catalyst in several years, given its status as one of the few major independent salty snack food companies in the U.S. market.

Risks: Where Could This Break?

Competitive intensity remains the most pressing threat. Private labels and major brands exert pressure on pricing and out-of-stock situations in growth markets. Leverage remains a concern, though it is controlled, thereby curbing flexibility if growth momentum changes. The risk of execution also persists, given its ongoing transformation of its network over several years. Service levels for its retail partners could have an immediate impact in case there are operational issues. Though it has "Better for You Products," changes in consumer views on health could demand faster innovation.

Conclusion: Still Worth the Price?

It’s not difficult to see why Utz is transforming from a regional snack food company into a national platform with enhanced branding, optimized networks, and growing margins. Fundamentally, the market’s valuation remains stuck in the old model, rather than recognizing improvements occurring in the underlying business. Overall, with good execution on its plan, I believe there’s attractive risk-reward in its shares, given its underlying improvements and its depressed valuation, prompting me to rate Utz BUY.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: One Of The Most Mispriced Alphas In The Market
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Joseph Elijah as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Thesis

Novo Nordisk A/S's (NVO) stock price has been in a downward spiral pretty much since the start of this year, with the stock down about 43% YTD. Now you would expect maybe something is wrong with company fundamentally but that's not the case here. This selloff started in March when their oral obesity drug trial results were on par with Eli Lilly not better which was expected. And then by the end of July they cut their guidance. The main reason was slow growth in the US market due to Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) taking some of its market share. Novo's stock kept tanking since then and on top of that, in August the Trump administration's drug pricing pressure kept adding fuel to the fire.

At this point the stock is down more than 65% from its all time highs all because they couldn't keep up with the sky-high expectation of the market. Now the market is also punishing Novo for slower growth in the US market (which I will talk about minute, and how it is about to reaccelerate). Right now much of the negative sentiments and adjusted-to-reality expectations are priced in, and I think that the bottom is in for Novo.

Everyone is so focused on the US market where Lilly's zepbound has gained ground with 53% share versus Novo's 46%. But give or take 85% to 90% of the diabetes and obesity patients live outside the US. And here's where it gets interesting in these international markets Novo is absolutely crushing it with over 72% market share and Wegovy sales growing 335% internationally in the first half of this year.

So while Wall Street's having a meltdown over US market share losses, they are completely missing the fact that Novo owns the rest of the world. The market's treating this like Novo's losing the war when in reality they are winning on multiple fronts that nobody's paying attention to. The global anti-obesity market is projected to be somewhere between $137 billion and $412 billion by 2034 at higher end. Out of 890 million people with obesity worldwide only about 4 million are on branded meds right now. That comes to about a 0.5% penetration rate, which means 99% of the market hasn't even been touched yet. And Novo is better positioned to capture this opportunity than Lilly because of their infrastructure in 80+ countries.

The CagriSema data that should have sent the stock up but instead tanked it doesn’t make sense to me. Because you are looking at about 22.7% weight loss with 67% coming from fat mass not muscle, cardio improvements that are better than Lilly and flexibility in dosing that could get us even better results. But the market was so focused on the headline number matching Lilly's that they missed all the other things underneath. Novo's trading at 13x forward earnings while Lilly's trading at 43x. Which is nearly a 4x valuation gap in the same space, except one has global dominance and the other's mainly a US story. Even if Novo just re-rates to half of Lilly's multiple you are looking at 100% upside from here.

REDFINE Trial Results

So let's start with what actually happened with the REDEFINE trials (Novo's Phase 3 trials for CagriSema) and I think that not only the market over reacted to the number, but also kind of misinterpreted it. Everyone saw that 22.7% weight loss number from REDEFINE 1 and immediately were like "it's just matching Lilly's tirzepatide, nothing special here," and completely ignored everything.

Here is what REDEFINE 1 actually showed. They enrolled 3417 patients with obesity and CagriSema delivered 22.7% mean weight loss in 68 weeks. But 40% of participants had more than 25% weight loss and 23% hit over 30% weight loss. Now if you compare that to Lilly's Surmount trials where only about 15% of patients hit that 30% mark. It's about 50% more patients achieving weight loss levels with CagriSema.

And that is not even the most impressive thing in this trial. The main point that I think is not appreciated enough and which should have sent the stock up 20% not down, is that when on Cagrisema, patients lost 35.7% of their total body fat mass while only losing 14.4% of lean muscle mass which means 67% of the weight loss was pure fat.

Most GLP-1s including tirzepatide see about 25 to 40% of weight loss coming from muscle, which is a huge problem doctors are worried about, especially in older patients. Because muscle recovery takes a lot of time, care and exercise, then it can cause a lot of problems, especially if the patient is above 50. Muscles are metabolically active and burn calories even at rest. So when you lose muscle mass, your basal metabolic rate decreases, making it easier to regain weight and harder to maintain weight loss long-term. They just basically solved one of the biggest problems in the obesity space.

On top of that they are also working on variable dosage for these drugs to reduce sensitivity and impact of the side effects, which I think may also expand their addressable market. So in REDEFINE 1, some patients had to reduce their doses because of side effects. Now you would expect these people to have worse weight loss outcomes.

But the patients on lower doses actually lost more weight. The ones who stayed on the full 2.4mg dose lost 22.2% at week 68, but the ones who had dose reductions lost over 25%. And, their GI adverse events dropped from an average of 1.9 per year to 4 per year on the lower doses, but they still had better weight loss. Now I don’t know why this happened but I guess that it could be because these patients had higher drug sensitivity overall which could have resulted in both strong GI effects and strong weight loss effects. But nevertheless it was great and a unique datapoint. This tells me that CagriSema might actually work better at personalized doses rather than the one size fits all approach everyone's been using so far.

The REDEFINE 2 trial in type 2 diabetes patients was equally good even though the market ignored it. These are typically harder to treat patients because diabetes meds often cause metabolic dysfunction and make weight loss tougher.

CagriSema delivered 15.7% weight loss with 51% of them having over 15% loss. Plus, (this is huge for diabetes treatment) 81% of patients got their HbA1c below 6.5% which is basically putting their diabetes in remission. Now based on all these superior results I think that not only the market overreacted but also misinterpreted the results.

This data only tells me one thing that if they come to the market with these drugs, due to their oral nature and the results of it in the global market, will have an adoption and penetration rate that would be at least 5 times higher than the injectables and it would more than offset their market share loss in the US.

Global Weight Loss Market Is Huge

I think that the main reason the Novo stock keeps tanking is the growth concerns in the US market. Yes, the US market is a premium market but it's not the only market in the world. And that’s why I see this reaction to the slow growth as an overreaction.

Because if you are focusing on the market share war between Novo and Lilly when there's as much as a $412 billion global obesity market staring them in the face, then you are looking at it the wrong way.

As I mentioned before, there are about 890 million people worldwide living with obesity. Right now, only 4 million are on branded GLP-1 meds. That's just a 0.5% penetration rate globally. So we are sitting here basically panicking about Novo losing a small market share in the US while most of the global market hasn't even been touched yet. Even if only a fraction of this population is eligible for these drugs, this still represents a huge potential market.

This market's growing at a 31% CAGR and may hit $412 billion by 2034. The US is maybe 15% of the global obesity population but accounts for over 60% of current GLP-1 sales, which means that the US market is already 10 times more penetrated than the rest of the world.

So, when a market gets that saturated customer acquisition costs go through the roof and growth slows to a crawl. This is what will happen in the US market over the next five years. The customer acquisition costs for the GLP-1 would rise so fighting for market share would cost more so instead it's a good idea to just go and get the rest of the world faster than the other which is exactly what Novo is doing.

They are operating in over 80 countries and their insulin business gave them the infrastructure that Lilly simply doesn't have.

You can't just show up in Indonesia or Nigeria and start selling GLP-1s. You need cold chain logistics and you need regulatory approvals. Novo's been building this for 40 years while Lilly's has been focused on the US market. So no matter how hard Novo tries Lilly will be ahead of Novo in the US market. So, Novo has a huge advantage over Lilly in the global market which is bigger than the US market.

As I said, Novo is trying to capture the global market which is far less competitive and bigger. They proved this already because the truth is in their numbers. In H1 2025, Novo's international Wegovy sales grew 335% and not to mention Wegovy is only available in 25 countries right now. They haven't even launched in India, Indonesia, or half of Latin America and they are having such a huge growth.

Wait until they really penetrate Southeast Asia where obesity rates are exploding. Malaysia's obesity rate went from 15% to 30% in just two decades. Thailand and the Philippines are all seeing the same trends. And In these markets Novo already dominates diabetes treatment with 70%+ market share in many countries.

China is often dismissed in this regard as a market due to economic headwinds, but the volume opportunity is huge there. They have the largest number of people with diabetes and obesity in the world.

The Chinese government recently launched the "Year of Weight Management" initiative. And Novo is now pivoting its strategy there. After seeing insulin prices slashed by Volume-Based Procurement (VBP) Novo is launching Wegovy in China with a focus on the "out-of-pocket" market first to protect margins. They are using their massive commercial footprint to penetrate private hospitals and direct-to-consumer channels before eventually negotiating volume deals.

On top of this, Southeast Asia and India have the fastest growing obesity rates globally. And to capture this market before Lilly In India, they recently did aggressive price cuts (up to 37% on Wegovy) to counter Lilly’s Mounjaro launch.

The upcoming Oral Semaglutide 25mg and Oral CagriSema would make sure that they have the highest market share in these markets. By removing the need for refrigeration they can bypass complex cold-chain logistics and sell these pills in regular pharmacies.

And this is the main reason why Novo is so focused on oral solutions for international expansion. Because in the US, patients are generally fine with injectables, they are used to it in the form of insulin, they have good refrigeration. But in emerging markets, the barriers to injectables are huge. Many homes don't have reliable refrigeration and the logistics of distributing and storing temperature sensitive injectables is a nightmare.

The oral semaglutide 25mg that's pending approval and oral CagriSema are the keys to these massive international markets. So they will be able to distribute GLP-1s through regular pharmacies without cold chain requirements. That would change everything.

Price points are another advantage internationally. While US insurers are paying $1000-1500 per month for GLP-1s, Novo can profitably sell these oral drugs for $200 per month in these markets and still maintain solid margins. Their manufacturing scale means they can hit price points that open up middle class populations in countries like Mexico, Brazil and Turkey. Lilly doesn't have the manufacturing footprint to compete at these price points profitably.

CEO Change Is Major Positive

The market viewed the CEO change very negatively, but I see it completely differently. Maziar Mike Doustdar taking over isn't the negative the market thinks it is. This guy ran international operations that delivered consistent 20%+ growth and understands global markets better than anyone in the industry. As I said earlier the majority of the obesity opportunity is outside the US. So having an internationally focused CEO is exactly what Novo needs.

Q3 Numbers Are Not As Bad As It Looks

So Novo just reported their Q3 earnings about a week ago and the market reacted to it with another selloff. The main reason was the growth because they narrowed guidance again and sales growth now expected at 8-11% and operating profit at 4-7% at constant rates.

Now I know that these numbers aren’t that good but this is also not the whole picture because if you strip out the one-time DKK 9 billion in restructuring costs operating profit actually grew 21% at CER.

I think that people are reacting too much to the temporary growth contraction and ignoring the market opportunity itself. This creates mispricing and opportunity for people who understand the market opportunity.

In Q3 their International Operations absolutely crushed it with 83% growth at CER while US Operations grew 24%. In the international market they are growing over three times faster than the US, yet everyone's focused on US market share.

The APAC region delivered 35% growth and that's with Wegovy barely launched in most Asian markets. Emerging Markets grew 22% and they haven't even touched most of Africa or Southeast Asia yet. Even China, with all its economic headwinds, delivered 6% growth and Novo maintained their 41% insulin market share there despite local competition. International Operations now are 44% of total company revenue up from just 35% two years ago.

That’s why I am so focused on the international market because Novo is killing it there and the current selloff is giving you an opportunity to buy this international growth for way cheaper.

Currently the sentiments are so pessimistic about Novo that I think that nobody is even pricing the upcoming catalysts that could surprise everyone and re-rate the stock.

First up, oral semaglutide 25mg decision is coming Q4 2025. If approved this would immediately expand the addressable market by millions of patients who won't touch a needle. The OASIS trials showed 16.6% weight loss with a pill, matching many injectable competitors. Then you have got CagriSema's Q1 2026 submission. Based on the REDEFINE data with 67% fat loss and superior cardio benefits, approval seems very likely to me.

If you look at where we are now versus where we were six months ago. The stock's down 43% YTD but the fundamentals have actually improved. They have got better visibility on manufacturing capacity. The pipeline's been significantly de-risked with positive REDEFINE data. And International expansion is accelerating faster than anyone expected.

And on top of that I think that all the bad news is now priced in. The market knows about the US competitive pressure, the compounding issues and the IRA pricing impact.

It's all reflected in that 13x P/E. But what's not priced in is the international growth, the pipeline, margin expansion from mix shift and that's about to show up once the restructuring is complete.

Novo is trading at its lowest valuation in 20 years while sitting on the biggest growth opportunity in healthcare. The current P/E of 13x is the lowest it's been in two decades. Over the past 20 years they have averaged a 23x P/E multiple.

Even if you strip out the GLP-1 hype years and just look at 2005 to 2020, before anyone knew what Ozempic was, they traded at 21x earnings. The market is basically pricing this like the obesity market is about to disappear tomorrow.

But on the other hand Eli Lilly is sitting at a 43x P/E multiple. That's nearly a 4x valuation gap for two companies in the exact same market.

Even on a forward basis looking out to 2028, Lilly trades at 20x earnings while Novo trades at just 11x. So the market thinks that even three years from now, after all the dust settles, Lilly deserves to trade at double Novo's multiple.

Novo's revenue is $33.8 billion TTM growing at 20.9% while Lilly's revenue is smaller but growing at 36.8%. Yes, Lilly's growing faster, but is 33% higher growth worth a 280% valuation premium. I don’t think so.

I also built a three stage DCF model to value Novo properly, and keep in mind that I am being conservative here because I don't want to get carried away, but even with these assumptions the upside is huge.

For Stage 1 (2025-2027), I am modeling 18% revenue growth which is actually below their current 21% growth rate. I am assuming EBITDA margins stay flat at around 51% even though they could expand with the restructuring. Management just announced DKK 8 billion in annualized savings from restructuring. That should add 2-3 percentage points to margins but I am ignoring it. Again, trying to be conservative here. For free cash flow, I am modeling it stays depressed at around 10% of revenue due to the massive CapEx they are doing.

Now in Stage 2 (2028-2032), I am assuming that the growth moderates to 12% as the market matures but margins expand as CapEx will normalize. The key here is that their free cash flow margin should jump from 10% to around 20% once they are done building out manufacturing capacity. Consensus already has them at a 16x EV/FCF by 2028, implying a 6% FCF yield and I think that's too conservative.

For terminal value I am using just 3.5% perpetual growth and a 20% FCF margin.

I am using an 8.5% WACC and honestly, that might be too high. Here's how I get there:

Cost of Equity:

  • Risk-free rate: 4.0% (US 10-year treasury)

  • Beta: 0.85

  • Market risk premium: 5.5%

  • Cost of equity: 4.0% + (0.85 × 5.5%) = 8.7%

Cost of Debt:

  • They have got DKK 15.7B in debt at about 3.5% interest

  • After-tax cost: 3.5% × (1 - 22% tax rate) = 2.73%

WACC:

  • Equity weight: 74% (market cap / enterprise value)

  • Debt weight: 26%

  • WACC: (74% × 8.7%) + (26% × 2.73%) = 7.1%

I am rounding up to 8.5% to be conservative. With US treasuries at 4% and their financial strength 8.5% is probably 150 basis points too high but whatever, I would rather be conservative. With a WACC of 8.5% this model yields us a fair value of $112 per share. Which is about 130% upside from current levels using assumptions that I think are borderline pessimistic.

If you apply a growth-adjusted multiple based on their relative growth rates, Novo should be trading at roughly 67% of Lilly's multiple, which would put their fair P/S ratio at 9.8x versus the current 4.9x. That alone suggests the stock should be trading around $96, which is basically double from here. If we just re-rate them to half of Lilly's current P/E multiple which would be a 26x P/E which would put the stock at $130. If it trades at its own historical average of 23x, that's $115.

Where I Could Be Wrong

Now I am not blind to the risks here. If CagriSema fails to gain approval or shows disappointment on a bigger scale in terms of efficacy compared to the trial data, it would eliminate my biggest near-term catalyst.

Unlike the US, where prices are market driven (though facing IRA caps starting 2026), many global markets impose strict controls that could squeeze Novo's margins.

In Europe, reference pricing ties costs to the lowest in a basket of countries. Their Wegovy has already faced cuts there about 20% since launch. Japan also implemented off-year price revisions in 2025 that could potentially cut prices by 15%.

While India's DPCO caps essential drugs at affordable levels it would possibly force Novo to sell orals below $100/month to qualify for government tenders. That’s why I was being so conservative in my DCF model because they will face pricing pressure but I don’t expect it to be that big because that would create an industry wide problem and maybe kill the market. So it's very unlikely.

While Lilly's Zepbound is the big US threat, but a wave of competition is starting globally, that would challenge Novo's 72% international share.

Viking Therapeutics Inc.'s (VKTX) VK2735 (oral GLP-1/GIP) showed 15% weight loss in Phase II with Phase III data expected Q1 2026 its affordability could disrupt the Asian market.

In China, local players like Innovent Biologics, Inc. (OTCPK:IVBXF) (mazdutide) are gaining traction with lower costs and government favoritism. If these outperform CagriSema in fat loss or dosing they could erode 10% of Novo's projected $50 billion+ international sales by 2030. But Novo's first-mover advantage, with Wegovy in 25 countries already, and superior data on muscle sparing, should help maintain leadership especially if everyone lacks on safety like Pfizer Inc. (PFE) did.

The compounding pharmacy issue in the US could go on longer than expected. While I believe regulatory action is coming in late 2025, delays could continue to pressure US market share. But I am not worried about this because the upcoming oral GLP-1s would counter this issue.

Final Thoughts

Novo Nordisk at current levels is the kind of opportunity that comes along maybe once or twice in a market cycle. The dominant market position, massive untapped global opportunity, near-term catalysts, make it what I believe to be one of the best risk-adjusted opportunities in the market.

The market's punishing them for slowing US growth while completely missing that they are about to capture the vast majority of a possibly $412 billion global opportunity. I think that when the market realizes that Novo's international strategy is actually superior to fighting for a small share in the US, this stock is going to rip higher.
